coredata.dc:title: Metal-doped carbon quantum dots for catalytic discoloration of methylene blue in day light
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Abu-Melha, Sraa
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) ingrained form biopolymers as photostable, biocompatible, and nontoxic fluorescence nanostructures, are superiorly applicable in different scientific disciplines. Attributing to their optoelectronic characters, CDs were interestingly exploited in environmental remediation as a catalyst under the effect of UV-light. However, the Current work represents a unique investigation for the superiority of metal-doped CDs (Pd@CDs & Ag@CDs ingrained from xanthan biopolymer) in catalytic discoloration of methylene blue (MB) under the effect of daylight. Geometrical features and mean size of CDs (7.5 nm), Pd@CDs (21.0 nm) & Ag@CDs (11.7 nm) were investigated via microscopic images and Zetasizer data. Additionally, CDs & Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs were exhibited with an observable peak at 220 nm and a broad band at 290 nm, to affirm the conjugative composition for all the nucleated dots. Catalytic performance of the prepared dots for reductive discoloration of MB in day light was followed the order of Pd@CDs > Ag@CDs ≫ CDs. Reduction percent was estimated to be 40 % for CDs, however after metal doping, the reduction percent was greatly enhanced to be 77 % and 83 %, for Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs, respectively. Kinetic studies showed that, the half time for discoloration of MB was decreased from 68.5 min with exploitation of CDs to 30.9 and 22.6 min, using Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Xanthan
Ag@CDs
Pd@CDs
Methylene Blue
Catalytic Discoloration

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CDs, Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs in MB discoloration in day light
Mechanism for nucleation of CDs and metal-doped CDs
Cds and metal doped CDs for probing catalytic discoloration of MB

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Deanship of Scientific Research, King Khalid University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University for funding this work through Small group Research Project under grant number: RGP1/77/44. Not applicable. The author(s) agree to publish the article under the Creative Commons Attribution License. All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article.
The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University for funding this work through Small group Research Project under grant number: RGP1/77/44.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorbance spectra, micro images and diffraction patterns for the synthesized samples; [a] absorbance, [b] CDs, [c] Pd@CDs and [d] Ag@CDs.
Particle size and zetapotential for the synthesized CDs and Pd@CDs and Ag@CDs; [a] Particle size, [b] zetapotential.
XRD results for the synthesized CDs and Pd@CDs and Ag@CDs.
Infrared spectra for the synthesized CDs and Pd@CDs and Ag@CDs.
Catalytic reduction of methylene blue in presence of the synthesized materials as catalyst; [a] absorbance at maximum wavelength (660 nm), [b] reduction percentage and [c] reduction mechanism.
Kinetic parameters for the catalytic reduction of methylene blue.

head.ce:title: Metal-doped carbon quantum dots for catalytic discoloration of methylene blue in day light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, King Khalid University, Abha 62529, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, King Khalid University, Abha 62529, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) ingrained form biopolymers as photostable, biocompatible, and nontoxic fluorescence nanostructures, are superiorly applicable in different scientific disciplines. Attributing to their optoelectronic characters, CDs were interestingly exploited in environmental remediation as a catalyst under the effect of UV-light. However, the Current work represents a unique investigation for the superiority of metal-doped CDs (Pd@CDs & Ag@CDs ingrained from xanthan biopolymer) in catalytic discoloration of methylene blue (MB) under the effect of daylight. Geometrical features and mean size of CDs (7.5 nm), Pd@CDs (21.0 nm) & Ag@CDs (11.7 nm) were investigated via microscopic images and Zetasizer data. Additionally, CDs & Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs were exhibited with an observable peak at 220 nm and a broad band at 290 nm, to affirm the conjugative composition for all the nucleated dots. Catalytic performance of the prepared dots for reductive discoloration of MB in day light was followed the order of Pd@CDs > Ag@CDs ≫ CDs. Reduction percent was estimated to be 40 % for CDs, however after metal doping, the reduction percent was greatly enhanced to be 77 % and 83 %, for Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs, respectively. Kinetic studies showed that, the half time for discoloration of MB was decreased from 68.5 min with exploitation of CDs to 30.9 and 22.6 min, using Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) ingrained from biopolymers as family of optically active, biocompatible and nontoxic nanostructures, to be superiorly applicable as alternatives for metallic dots and organic colorants in different purposes such as, electronics, chemical or biosensing, and biomedical fields [1–4]. Moreover, CDs exhibit superior characters, like, resistant to photo-bleaching, cheapness and simple synthetic methodologies and easy for bio-conjugation [5]. Numerous recent approaches were considerably interested in investigation of different simple/unique/easy synthetic techniques for CDs with their exploitation in different purposes [6–9].

Under the effect of ultraviolet light, exposing of CDs to photons of energy that is equal or higher than its band gap results in transferring of an electron from valence band to conduction band, giving an electron-hole. Both of hole and electron on CDs surface make it able for acting as either electron donor or acceptor, to promote the oxidation–reduction on the surface of the exploited dot [10]. Metal doping for CDs was investigated to induce a shift in the luminescence wavelength, to be applicable as a highly efficient photocatalyst (i.e., under the effect of UV light). Different semiconductors were reported to be successfully applicable as photocatalyst (i.e., under the effect of UV-light) for degradation of organic pollutants and microorganisms [11]. In another report, it was approved that, the photocatalytic activities of CDs can be significantly improved via the incorporation of metals such as titanium dioxide [12]. However, according to our knowledge, no previous reports were investigated the effect of doping metals in activate CDs to act as superior catalyst in daylight (i.e., in visible light).

CDs Doped with inorganic metals were reported to exhibit significantly enhanced functionalization and optical characters [13]. Doping of zinc oxide or zinc sulfide within CDs was carried out via alkaline hydrolysis or precipitation with Na2S, with dispersion in zinc acetate. Whereas, the prepared ZnO@CDs and ZnS@CDs were characterized with strong bluish/green florescence emission, while, the mechanism was suggested to be attributed to the surface passivation akin to the organic passivation reagent [13]. Sun et al, also synthesized green florescent CDs via doping ZnS and TiO2 [14]. Recently, zinc-doped CDs with strong yellow florescence was successfully synthesized using Zn+2 ions and citric acid as the molecular precursors under solvothermal conditions [15]. Additionally, Mn doped and Rb-doped CDs with different color emissions were also reported [16–18].

On the other hand, it is well known that, organic dyes are widely applicable in different industries like food, pharmacy, cosmetics, paints, plastics, paper and textiles. However, synthetic dyes are approved to be hazardous for both of human health and the environment as well. Numerous conventional treatment methods such like coagulation, filtration, adsorption and reverse osmosis were exploited for dye discoloration and removal from wastewater. Nevertheless, it was reported that, their elimination from wastewater is not so easy, because of their stable aromatic composition. Recently, nano-catalysts become one of the recently applied reagents for the reductive discoloration of dyes [19–25].

Xanthan gum as anionic polyelectrolytic biopolymer, is one of the microbial exo-polysaccharides that mainly composed of a cellulosic skeleton with two mannose and one glucuronic acid as side chains [26,27]. Xanthan gum exists as an extremely viscous solution even at very low concentrations, due to its high molecular weight, that might reach up to 6 million Daltons [28]. Additionally, xanthan gum is stable over a wide range of temperatures and pH, attributing to its enzymatic resistance, to make it widely applicable in various purposes such as, food industry, pharmaceutics, cosmetics, and oil-drilling industrialization [29–32].

The demonstrated approach represents unique strategy for application of metal CDs in environmental remediation. The point of novelty in the represented approach is the nucleation of silver doped CDs (Ag@CDs) & palladium doped CDs (Pd@CDs), to be successfully exploited for discoloration of methylene blue (MB) in daylight. Meaning that, the main goal of our study is to highlight the effect of doping either silver or palladium in enhancement of catalytic action of CDs to be applicable in daylight. Whereas, CDs were formerly ingrained form xanthan biopolymer. Successive ingraining of Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs was confirmed via TEM, Zetasizer, zetapotential, FTIR & XRD analysis. The synthesized Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs were sequentially exploited in catalytic discoloration of MB in day light. The mechanism for the catalytic action of Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs was also schematically presented. Attributing to the optoelectronic characters of the prepared Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs, they could be superiorly applied in environmental remediation studies as alternatives to metal semiconductors for removal of different colorful water pollutants.

Xanthan (xanthan gum fromXanthomonas campestris, 25–70 mPa.s, 1 % in H2O, Sigma-Aldrich), Silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.9 %, from Panreac, Barcelona – Spain), palladium chloride (PdCl2, ≥99.9 %, Sigma-Aldrich), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥98 %, Sigma-Aldrich), methylene blue (MB, C.I.13020, C15H15N3O2, Merck) and sodium borohydride (NaBH4, ≥96 %, Sigma-Aldrich), were all exploited without any further purification.

CDs were simply nucleated from xanthan as follows; 2 g/l of xanthan was alkali hydrolyzed with 20 g/l of NaOH, to be kept under magnetic stirring at 90 ℃ for 2 h. For complete nucleation of CDs, the produced nano-colloid was transferred in a vertical autoclavable cup, to put it in a muffle at 160 °C for half day, for hydrothermal treatment. Afterward, the reaction liquor with dark yellow color was cooled at ambient conditions to be dialyzed with distilled water for day night (pur-A-lyzer dialysis kit, MWCO 6–8 kDa from Sigma-Aldrich). However, for preparation of metal doped CDs (Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs), either silver salt or palladium salt was formerly added to the alkali hydrolyzed xanthan before the hydrothermal treatment.

For Characterization of CDs, Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs, High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope from Japan JEOL-JEM-1200″ was exploited for determination of the topography and geometrical features. Absorption spectral data for CDs, Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs were manifested at 250–750 nm (spectrophotometer “Cary 100 UV–VIS, UV–Vis-NIR Systems“, from Agilent). The mean particle size and zetapotential for the prepared CDs Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs were evaluated via Zetasizer analyzer with dynamic light scattering technique at 25 °C in insulated chamber (Malvern Nano ZS, from Malvern Instruments Ltd – UK). The absorbance was estimated at 500 – 4000 cm−1 (15 points smoothing, 4 cm−1 resolution, 64 scanning times with scanning rate of 2 mm.sec −1). The optical properties for the prepared CDs, Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs in ultraviolet–visible light were monitored via spectro-florometer “JASCO FP8300”, whereas, the data collected at room temperature with excitation at 340 nm. Additionally, CDs, Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs were characterized with powder X-ray diffraction using X’Pert MPD diffractometer system (Philips, at room temperature), whereas, diffraction bands were collected in the diffraction angle (2θ) of 3.5–50° using monochromator (Cu Kα X-radiation at 40 kV, 50 mA and λ = 1.5418 Å). Infrared spectral data for CDs, Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs were represented using “Jasco FT/IR 6100 spectrometer”. ”Jeol‐Ex‐300 NMR spectrometry, JEOL – Japan“ was used for plotting 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectral maps for the prepared dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs, Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs in MB discoloration in day light
ce:section.ce:para: The main goal of our study is to highlight the effect of doping either silver or palladium in enhancement of catalytic action of CDs in day light. Therefore, a comparable study is currently presented for approving the catalytic affinity in day light for CDs versus Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs, for the discoloration of methylene blue (MB). The discoloration process of MB in daylight was performed using sodium borohydride as a reducing agent in presence of either CDs or metal doped CDs (Ag@CDs or Pd@CDs) as a catalyst. To 25 mL of dye (6 mg/L), a definite volume (0.5 mL) of the tested dots colloid was accurately added, taking in account that, the total added concentration of dots colloid is 150 mg, whatever the composition of nano-colloid. Afterwards, a certain volume of sodium borohydride (1 mL, 2 mM) was dropped. The progression of the dye discoloration was monitored via the absorbance spectra. An aliquot from the mixture solution was withdrawn at various interval time (0–90 min) and the absorbance spectra were collected using Cary 100 UV–VIS spectrophotometer (UV–Vis-NIR Systems, from Agilent).

All the presented data in the current work was figured out by using excel of Microsoft 2016. The degradation experiments of methylene blue dye were conducted in the means for three experiments and the all kinetic parameters were calculated from excel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for nucleation of CDs and metal-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, xanthan as a biocompatible/nontoxic/abundant biopolymer was exploited for clustering CDs and metal-doped CDs (Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs). Xanthan macromolecules were alkali hydrolyzed for enhancement of its accessibility to produce the desirable size/shape regulated CDs. For successive nucleation of CDs, it could be hypothesized that; under the hydrothermal conditions, the alkali hydrolyzed xanthan macromolecules are suggested to be degraded for de-polymerization, re-polymerization, oxidation and eventual aromatization, to give size and shape controllable CDs, decorated with heteroatom containing functional groups [33–35]. In case of metal doped-CDs (Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs), according to literature [36], nanosized metals were nucleated formerly by the action of the alkali hydrolyzed xanthan to be successively doped within CDs that were subsequently clustered under hydrothermal conditions.

UV–Visible spectroscopic mapping for CDs, Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs that were nucleated from xanthan biopolymer are represented in Fig. 1
a. From the showed data, it could be declared that, all of the examined CDs were shown with with sharp peaks at 210 nm that is corresponding to π - π* transition (conjugation in aromatic clusters), and a broad peak at 320 nm with long extension in visible range, signing for n- π* transition (electron radiative relaxing, for decorative groups as heteroatom containing groups), respectively [8,37]. These findings approved that, the as-nucleated CDs were exhibited by surface decoration with the heteroatom containing functional groups [6,38].

Topographical and geometrical features of the prepared CDs and metal-doped CDs (Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs) could be obviously investigated from the plotted microscopic images of Transmission Electron Microscope in Fig. 1 b, 1c & 1d, moreover, the estimated size distribution for each was also demonstrated according to Zetasizer analysis (Fig. 2
a). From the plotted data, xanthan was successfully exploited in nucleation of CDs, Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs with contrallable size and geometry, whereas, the mean size was estimated to be 7.5 nm, 11.7 nm & 21.0 nm, respectively. Meaning that, both of silver and palladium were served as a supporting template for clustering smaller sized CDs, however, Pd was exhibited with higher affinity for nucleation of smaller sized CDs rather than Ag, which is in harmony with literature [39], whereas, palladium exhibits higher chemical reactivity and smaller surface area. So, it could be depicted that, doping of metals served in successive nucleation of size and shape regulated CDs.

On the other hand, Fig. 2 b showed the results of Zeta-potential analysis for CDs before and after metal doping. Zeta-potential is a key parameter for identification of the degree of electrostatic repulsive interaction between neighboring similarly charged particles in a colloidal solution. Negative value of Zeta-potential refers to the electrostatic forces that is expressed as repulsive forces between the dispersed particles in the colloid, i.e., the larger is the estimated negative value, meaning that, the repulsion between the dispersed particles is greater, which in turn reflected in higher stabilized dispersion [40]. According to literature, the smaller negative value of zeta potential is attributed to the particle agglomeration as a result of weak van der Waals attractive forces, and these in turn result in physical instability [41]. From the plotted data in Fig. 2 b, it could be declared that, the colloidal solution of CDs that was synthesized with alkaline hydrolysis of xanthan under hydrothermal conditions was exhibited by the highest negatively charged value of zeta-potential (-28 mV), whereas, observable decrement in the estimated value was observed after metal doping, to be −20 mV & −10 mV, for Ag@CDs and Pd@CDs, respectively, attributing to the effect of metal doping in enlarging particle size as previously mentioned in size estimation data. However, doping of silver could undesirably affect on the stabilization of CDs colloid rather than palladium, due to larger sized CDs with doping of silver. So, it could be summarized that, doping of palladium could be more beneficial for clustering of smaller sized and higher stabilized CDs than silver, that is in harmony with the illustrated data of topographical features and particle size.

Fig. 3 represents XRD analysed data, to show that, alkali hydrolysed xanthan gum is largely amorphous and had only two crystalline peaks in the 2θ = 10° & 20° [42]. Whereas, after exposing to hydrothermal treatment for complete clustering of CDs, the characteristic peaks of CDs were estimated at 2θ° = 24.9°, 37.5°, 43.8°, 64.2° and 77.4°. successive metal doping within CDs matrix resulted in obvious observation of the characteristic bands of crystalline Pd and Ag, and by referring to diffraction data of international center, the diffraction peak at 2θ = 38.1° typical for (111) of face centered crystalline (FCC) structures for silver (JCPDS data number 04–0783 card and 4–0784 card) [43–45]. Whereas, the diffraction pattern at 74.9° is known to be typical for (220) of face centered crystalline (FCC) structures for Pd [46,47]. These could affirm the high affinity of the demonstrated method for nucleation of highly stable/crystalline quantum dots, to be successfully applied in the desirable purposes.

From 1HNMR ( Fig.S1 ), xanthan was exhibited with typical peaks for proton of sp3 at 1–3 ppm and proton of hydroxyl decorative groups at 2.82 ppm ( Fig.S1
a). Whereas, after exploitation of xanthan in nucleation of CDs ( Fig.S1
b), additional significant peaks for protons attached to carbonyl groups and protons attached to sp2 carbons, at 3.6 ppm and 8.5 ppm, respectively. However, after metal doping ( Fig.S1
c & d) only one peak of protons bonded to carbonyl group was estimated. Moreover, 13CNMR spectra of xanthan also showed that, one band at 40 ppm corresponds to carbon bonded to OH ( Fig.S2
a). Whereas, after its exploitation in CDs ingraining before and after metal doping ( Fig.S2
b, c & d), an additional peak at 169 ppm assigns to sp2 carbon was notified. These findings mean that, metal doping did not affect on the aromatic character of the as-prepared CDs, that is in harmony with all-above illustrated data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cds and metal doped CDs for probing catalytic discoloration of MB
ce:section.ce:para: Water contamination is a one of the serious worldwide problems [49]. Numerous synthetic colorants are exploited for dyeing industries. Ten percentage of dyes from the textile industries are mainly discharged to the environment and aquatic systems. Such toxic dyes as organic water pollutants as well-known as carcinogenic compounds and mutagenic for marine organisms and human beings [50]. Whereas, it causes a number of biological disorders, intestinal tumors and affects the seeding of the plant [51]. Recently, some reports were interested in the investigation of efficient techniques for wastewater treatment and removal or degradation of hazardous colored chemicals to lesser toxic fragments. Different techniques were applied such as biodegradation [52], photo-degradation [53], electro-coagulation [54], adsorption [55] and chemical oxidation [56]. The most recent reports were extensively considered with the exploitation of nanostructured reagents for dyes removal and degradation [19–22].

The catalytic performance of the as-prepared CDs before and after metal doping in the discoloration of MB is currently studied, via monitoring their effect in the acceleration of the reductive discoloration of MB by sodium borohydride as a reducing agent, and the data were plotted in Fig. 5 and Supplementary data. MB is a basic aniline dye that is also known as methyl thioninium chloride, and it has different applications in various fields of biology, chemistry and in medicine [57–59]. The oxidized state of MB is blue in color, whereas, its reduced form is colorless leuco-compound. UV–Visible absorption spectrum of reduction for MB using sodium borohydride as a reducing agent, in the absence and presence of CDs, before and after metal doping, are shown in Supplementary data ( Figs. S3
&
Fig. S4 ). Also, Fig. 5 a represents the relationship between absorbance at λmax with discoloration time, while Fig. 5 b showed the effect of time on reduction percentage of MB using the as-prepared quantum dots.

MB was typically shown with an absorption maxima band at 664 nm referring to п / п* and n / п* transitions [60], whereas, the progress in reductive discoloration can be easily approved via monitoring the decrement in the height of absorption band. The degradation of MB in the dark was performed in Fig. S3 for comparison with that catalytic reduction in visible light. The obtained data notified that only 5.4 % from MB was degraded in the dark within 30 min. While, the degradation percentage of MB reached 27.4 % and 33.6 % in case of Ag@CDs and Pd@CDs, respectively. however, the degradation percentage in the dark was insufficient and significantly lowered compared to with that in the visible light. In case of applying the visible light, the reduction percent in the absence of the as-prepared quantum dots still only 20 % after 30 min, while, by exploitation of the synthesized quantum dots as nano-catalyst, the reduction percent is gradually increased, iii) by comparing between CDs (reductive discoloration percent 45 %), Ag@CDs (70 %) and Pd@CDs (83 %), it could be depicted that, doping of CDs with palladium showed to maximize the catalytic performance of CDs for maximum discoloration of MB after 30 min.

Kinetic studies for the reductive discoloration reaction of MB, in the absence and presence of the currently prepared CDs, with and without metal doping, are performed and the evaluated key parameters were tabulated in Table 1 and in Supplementary data ( Fig. S5 ). Form the estimated data, it could be declared that, the reductive discoloration reaction of MB the presence of CDs before and after metal doping is first ordered reaction, i.e., the concentration of the exploited CDs is the main parameter that affects on the rate of reaction. Moreover, without metal doping, CDs is shown with the lowest estimated K constant (10.1 × 103 1/sec) and the highest t1/2 (68.5 min) values, whereas, metal doping resulted in significant increment of K constant and decrement in t1/2, as Ag@CDs is exhibited with significantly higher K constant value and lower t1/2 to be 21.0 × 103 1/sec and 30.9 min, respectively. However, Pd@CDs is shown with the highest estimated K constant (25.1 × 103 1/sec) and lowest t1/2 (22.6 min). Meaning that, doping of palladium within CDs resulted in nucleation of quantum dots with the highest catalytic performance, attributing to the highest catalytic activity of palladium [39,61].

The reaction mechanism for catalytic discoloration of MB by using the as-prepared quantum dots could be hypothesized as follows (Fig. 5 c); BDE (bond dissociation energy) is a key parameter in a certain chemical reaction for breaking and/or building up of new bond, whereas, electron transferring occurs during the reaction between the dye and the reducer (NaBH4), as the reducer acts as an electron donating site and MB as electron accepting site [62]. Whereas, the as-prepared quantum dots could superiorly act as a potential intermediate between MB & borohydride ions (BH4 − ions) for lowering BDE for accelerating the electron transferring. Moreover, the rate of reduction of MB by NaBH4 in the presence of Pd@CDs was maximized, owing to the highest chemical reactivity of palladium compared to silver, and as it exhibits smaller size, i.e., larger surface area, to be reflected in the highest catalytic performance.

The demonstrated approach represents unique strategy for application of metal CDs in environmental remediation. The main goal of our study is to highlight the effect of doping either silver or palladium in enhancement of catalytic action of CDs in day light. Meanwhile, the point of novelty in the represented approach is the nucleation of silver doped CDs (Ag@CDs) & palladium doped CDs (Pd@CDs), to be successfully exploited for discoloration of methylene blue (MB) in daylight. Whereas, CDs were formerly ingrained form xanthan biopolymer. Successive ingraining of Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs was confirmed via TEM, Zetasizer, zetapotential, FTIR & XRD analysis. The synthesized Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs were sequentially exploited in catalytic discoloration of MB. The mechanism for the catalytic action of Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs was also schematically presented. The represented data showed that, the catalytic performance of the prepared dots for reductive discoloration of MB in daylight was followed the order of Pd@CDs ≫ Ag@CDs > CDs, whereas, the reduction percent was duplicated after metal doping. Attributing to the optoelectronic characters of the prepared Ag@CDs & Pd@CDs, they could be superiorly applied in environmental remediation as alternatives to metal semiconductors for removal of different colorful water pollutants. Therefore, the represented technique could be expressed as an economic and green method of environmental remediation.
Sraa Abu-Melha: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University for funding this work through Small group Research Project under grant number: RGP1/77/44.

ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable.
The author(s) agree to publish the article under the Creative Commons Attribution License.
All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.115233.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.N. Baker, G.A. Baker, Luminescent carbon nanodots: emergent nanolights, Angewandte Chemie International Edition 49(38) (2010) 6726-6744.
O. Kozak, M. Sudolska, G. Pramanik, P. Cigler, M. Otyepka, R. Zboril, Photoluminescent carbon nanostructures, Chemistry of Materials 28(12) (2016) 4085-4128.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots—Emerging light emitters for bioimaging, cancer therapy and optoelectronics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Hola, Y. Zhang, Y. Wang, E.P. Giannelis, R. Zboril, A.L. Rogach, Carbon dots—Emerging light emitters for bioimaging, cancer therapy and optoelectronics, Nano Today 9(5) (2014) 590-603.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.T. Zheng, A. Ananthanarayanan, K.Q. Luo, P. Chen, Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications, small 11(14) (2015) 1620-1636.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis, characterization and cells and tissues imaging of carbon quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wang, Q. Li, J. Zhou, Y. Wang, L. Yu, H. Peng, J. Zhu, Synthesis, characterization and cells and tissues imaging of carbon quantum dots, Optical Materials 72 (2017) 15-19.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Technical textiles modified with immobilized carbon dots synthesized with infrared assistance

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recyclable photocatalyst composites based on Ag 3 VO 4 and Ag 2 WO 4@ MOF@ cotton for effective discoloration of dye in visible light

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.E. Emam, H.B. Ahmed, E. Gomaa, M.H. Helal, R.M. Abdelhameed, Recyclable photocatalyst composites based on Ag 3 VO 4 and Ag 2 WO 4@ MOF@ cotton for effective discoloration of dye in visible light, Cellulose (2020) 1-17.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antitumor/antiviral carbon quantum dots based on carrageenan and pullulan

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.E. Emam, H.B. Ahmed, Antitumor/antiviral carbon quantum dots based on carrageenan and pullulan, International Journal of Biological Macromolecules 170 (2021) 688-700.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Affinity of carbon quantum dots anchored within metal organic framework matrix as enhancer of plant nourishment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.B. Ahmed, N.E. Mahmoud, A.A. Mahdi, H.E. Emam, R.M. Abdelhameed, Affinity of carbon quantum dots anchored within metal organic framework matrix as enhancer of plant nourishment, Heliyon 8(12) (2022).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescent carbon nanodots: synthesis, physicochemical properties and analytical applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in carbon–semiconductor nanocomposites for water remediation
